title: Liftoff
narrationScript: Lift-off is the impedance change produced when the spacing between a surface coil and the part changes, and it is the strongest, fastest signal most surface probes ever generate. Coupling falls off steeply, almost exponentially, so the first tenth of a millimetre of gap moves the dot more than the next half millimetre does. On the impedance plane, every lift-off locus leaves the operating point and heads for the air point, and the angle between lift-off and a crack response opens as frequency pushes the operating point down the conductivity curve. That angle is your working room. Null on the part, lift onto a thin shim, rotate the trace flat, and evaluate the vertical channel. Then prove your notch response still survives the maximum expected coating. Lift-off is also a tool. Hold the coil off the metal with a nonconductive coating, and the dot's position along the lift-off locus reads coating thickness, calibrated with certified shims on bare base metal under ASTM E376.

sections.heading: Lift-Off: The Coupling Variable You Touch Most
sections.body:
**Lift-off** is the impedance change produced when the spacing between a surface coil and the test surface varies — ASTM E1316 frames the liftoff effect as a change in test-system output caused by a change in **magnetic coupling** between probe and part as their separation changes. It is the surface-probe twin of the fill factor you studied in the last lesson: identical physics, flat geometry instead of encircling.

The coil's field is densest right at its face, so coupling collapses **steeply and nonlinearly** — close to exponentially — as the gap opens:

- The first **0.05–0.1 mm** of separation moves the dot farther than the next half millimetre does.
- A surface coil runs out of useful coupling within a gap on the order of its **own diameter** — for a 3-mm pencil-probe coil, a couple of millimetres of air is electrical infinity.
- Anything that changes the effective gap writes the signal: hand wobble, probe **tilt** (lift-off across one side of the coil face), paint and primer thickness variation, scale, surface roughness, and curvature the probe cannot follow.

Why Level II owns this variable: in surface scanning, lift-off is almost always the **largest noise source on the screen**. A few hundredths of a millimetre of hand wobble can rival the response of the crack you were hired to find. Every surface-probe setup you sign will therefore handle lift-off twice — suppress it where it is noise, and read it where it is measurement.

sections.caption: Every lift-off locus leaves its operating point and ends at the air point — and the phase separation Δφ between lift-off and the conductivity/defect directions opens as the operating point moves down the curve.

sections.heading: Amplitude and Phase: How the Dot Moves
sections.body:
Seat an absolute surface probe on a nonmagnetic part and coupling is at maximum: eddy currents are strongest, their secondary field depresses the coil's inductive reactance and raises its effective resistance, and the dot sits at the material's **operating point** on the conductivity curve. Now open the gap and watch both halves of the signal.

- **Amplitude:** the dot retraces along the **lift-off locus** toward the **air point** — normalized reactance climbs back toward 1 while resistance falls away. Equal increments of gap produce **successively smaller excursions**: sensitivity is greatest at contact and dies off as the probe rises, which is exactly why near-contact wobble is so loud.
- **Phase:** at the operating point, the lift-off direction differs from the conductivity direction — and from defect responses — by an angle set by **where the operating point sits**. Near the top of the curve the directions nearly coincide; move down the curve (raise the frequency, or test a higher-conductivity metal) and the separation opens.

Frequency selection is therefore **angle selection**. For aluminum surface-crack work, the test frequency is chosen to push the separation between lift-off and the crack response toward a usable maximum — ideally approaching 90° — so a crack still stands up after lift-off is laid flat.

Two extensions a Level II must carry. On **ferromagnetic** parts the operating point sits *above* the air point (permeability raises reactance), so the lift-off locus runs *downward* to the air point and tangles with permeability noise — magnetic saturation, next lesson, is the cure. And in **bobbin-coil tube testing** the same coupling physics appears as fill factor: **probe wobble** is lift-off by another name, and it is the classic noise source in tube data.

sections.title: Lift-off: enemy and tool
sections.body: The same locus does two jobs. **Enemy:** when you hunt cracks, lift-off is the dominant noise — null on the part, rotate the lift-off excursion onto the horizontal axis, and evaluate the vertical channel. **Tool:** when you measure nonconductive coating thickness, lift-off *is* the signal — the dot's position along the locus reads the gap directly. One curve, two jobs: suppression and measurement.

sections.heading: Compensation: Rotation, Vertical Evaluation, Hardware
sections.body:
The standard surface-scan compensation is **phase rotation**, and it is defined by a controlled excursion — never by eye:

- **Null on the part**, away from edges and fasteners, with the probe seated flat.
- **Lift onto a nonconductive shim** of defined thickness — 0.1 mm is typical; use the maximum expected coating thickness when paint is in play.
- **Rotate the display** until the excursion runs horizontal, conventionally leaving the null to the left. Re-seat and confirm the trace hugs the baseline.
- **Evaluate and alarm on the vertical (Y) component.** Box gates and threshold alarms watch Y; the X channel absorbs the wobble.

Two cautions before you trust it. The full lift-off locus is **curved**, so only the near-contact working portion can be made flat — re-set the rotation after any probe, cable, or frequency change. And vertical-only evaluation **discards every signal component parallel to lift-off**: a defect whose phase lies near the lift-off direction loses amplitude or vanishes. Prove the separation on your reference standard's EDM notches — through a shim equal to the maximum coating — before the first scan, not after the last one.

Hardware carries the rest. **Spring-loaded probe bodies** hold contact force constant. **Wear caps** add a small *constant* standoff, which is acceptable because it is constant — null and standardize with the cap installed. Templates and **circle guides** steady the path around fastener heads, and conformable shoes manage curvature. Meter-type crack detectors and conductivity instruments carry built-in **lift-off compensation** — an operating point chosen so small gap changes barely move the reading — and in automated rotating-scanner and tube work, wobble's low signal frequency lets **filtering** and multifrequency mixing strip it away from the sharp defect channel.

sections.caption: Phase rotation with a defined excursion: the 0.1 mm shim signal is laid onto the horizontal axis, leaving the crack response to rise into the vertical alarm channel.

sections.heading: Lift-Off as a Tool: Coating Thickness
sections.body:
A nonconductive coating is **frozen lift-off**: it holds the coil exactly its own thickness off the metal. Calibrate that relationship and the impedance plane becomes a micrometer for paint and anodize.

The governing practice is **ASTM E376**, and its discipline is the lesson:

- **Calibrate on the bare base metal** — same alloy and temper (conductivity moves the locus), same curvature, and base metal thicker than the effective depth of penetration or backed consistently.
- **Use certified nonconductive shims** spanning the expected range, zeroed at contact, with verification points across the range and periodic re-checks.
- **Respect geometry:** stay away from edges, and calibrate on the curvature you will measure — a convex surface does not read like the flat plate that calibrated you.
- **Work the steep end.** The response is steep for thin coatings and flattens as the gap grows, so resolution is best exactly where aircraft paint systems live — tenths of a millimetre. Relatively high test frequencies maximize lift-off sensitivity for this job.

The same thinking guards your conductivity work. **ASTM E1004** permits %IACS measurement through a thin nonconductive coating only within the instrument's demonstrated **lift-off compensation**: place a shim equal to the maximum coating on the bare conductivity standard and show the reading shift stays inside procedure limits. The spec sheet claims compensation; your standardization proves it.

sections.caption: A nonconductive coating is frozen lift-off: calibrated with certified shims on the bare base metal, the dot's travel along the lift-off locus reads coating thickness — steep and precise when thin, flattening as the gap grows.
Where lift-off shows up in the field — and the working countermeasure.
rows: Free-hand scanning for surface cracks
Baseline wander along the rotated horizontal axis
Spring-loaded probe, steady pressure, lift-off rotated flat, Y-channel evaluation
Paint/primer thickness varies along a lap joint
Slow drift — and sensitivity loss through thick paint
Null mid-joint; prove notch response through a shim equal to maximum coating
Probe wear protection (wear cap)
Small constant standoff and some signal loss
Acceptable because constant — null and standardize with the cap installed
Curved or rough surfaces
Effective gap varies across the coil face (tilt)
Matched shoes and guides; probe size per procedure; standardize on like geometry
Scanning around fastener heads
Wobble loops that mimic flaw signals
Circle templates and guides; compare every call against the reference standard
Bobbin coil in tubing
Probe wobble — the fill-factor/lift-off analog
Well-fitted probe and centering devices; differential mode and filtering suppress slow wobble
Coating thickness measurement
Lift-off is the measurand
Certified shims on bare base metal per ASTM E376; work the steep end of the curve

figures.title: Lift-off on the normalized impedance plane

interactive.title: Painted Lap Joint: Lift-Off in the Field
interactive.intro: You are scanning a painted aluminum lap joint for surface cracks at the fastener rows — shielded absolute surface probe, 200 kHz, impedance-plane display. Work each decision before you click.

steps.prompt: Frequency and gain are set. The procedure requires lift-off compensation. Your next action:
options: Null in air, then rotate until the dot stops moving
Null on the part away from fasteners, lift onto a 0.1 mm shim, and rotate the excursion horizontal
Rotate the display 90° first, then null wherever the dot lands
Skip rotation — the shielded probe is immune to lift-off
steps.feedback: Rotation is defined by a controlled excursion from an on-part null: null seated flat, lift onto the shim, rotate the trace flat (convention: the dot exits left). Shielding narrows the field's footprint; it does not remove the coupling's dependence on gap.

steps.prompt: Scanning the row, the dot wanders left and right along the baseline; the vertical channel stays quiet. What are you seeing?
options: Multiple shallow cracks
Conductivity variation in the sheet
Paint-thickness variation and hand wobble — compensated lift-off doing exactly what it should
Instrument drift that requires re-nulling before each reading
steps.feedback: Horizontal wander with a quiet Y channel is the rotated lift-off signature — paint and wobble ride the X axis. Cracks or conductivity changes would put energy into the vertical component.

steps.prompt: At one fastener a sharp signal rises steeply into the vertical channel and repeats on re-scan. Your move as a Level II:
options: Re-scan and compare its amplitude and phase against the EDM-notch response on the reference standard before classifying
Reduce the gain until the signal stays below the alarm
Ignore it — vertical signals near fasteners are always geometry
Mark the part rejected; no comparison needed
steps.feedback: Evaluation means comparison against standardization: repeatability, then amplitude and phase against known notches. Turning gain down to silence an indication is falsification; auto-rejecting without comparison is almost as bad.

steps.prompt: Paint on this joint can reach 0.25 mm, but your notch standard is bare metal. Before reporting the joint clean, you must:
options: Do nothing — rotation already compensated for the paint
Sand a witness strip of paint off every joint
Double the gain across the whole inspection
Demonstrate the notch still gives an evaluable vertical response through a 0.25 mm shim on the standard
steps.feedback: Rotation hides the lift-off signal; it does not restore the sensitivity that the coating's standoff costs you. The shim demonstration proves detection through maximum coating — the difference between a clean report and a hopeful one.

checkQuestions.question: A surface probe on a nonmagnetic part lifts slowly away from the surface. On the normalized impedance plane, the operating point:
options: Slides down the conductivity curve toward the copper end
Moves straight toward the origin (0, 0)
Retraces along the lift-off locus toward the air point — normalized reactance rises toward 1 while resistance falls
Stays fixed, because probe spacing is mechanical rather than electrical
checkQuestions.explanation: Opening the gap weakens magnetic coupling, so the coil's impedance returns toward its empty-coil values: the dot travels the lift-off locus, and every such locus ends at the air point.

checkQuestions.question: Equal 0.05 mm increments of additional lift-off produce:
options: Successively smaller signal excursions — sensitivity is greatest near contact and decays steeply with gap
Equal excursions, since the increments are equal
Successively larger excursions as the coil approaches its air value
No change until the probe fully leaves the part's magnetic field
checkQuestions.explanation: Coupling falls off nonlinearly — close to exponentially — with spacing. The first fraction of a millimetre moves the dot most, which is why near-contact wobble is the loudest noise in surface scanning.

checkQuestions.question: The standard setup for suppressing lift-off noise during impedance-plane surface scanning is:
options: Null in air and reduce the gain until wobble disappears
Select the lowest available test frequency so lift-off vanishes
Filter out every signal below the alarm threshold in both channels
Null on the part, lift onto a thin nonconductive shim, rotate the excursion onto the horizontal axis, and evaluate the vertical component
checkQuestions.explanation: Rotation is set with a defined on-part excursion so lift-off rides the X axis while defect responses retain a vertical component. Gain starvation and frequency tricks sacrifice the defect signal along with the noise — lift-off never vanishes.

checkQuestions.question: After rotating lift-off horizontal and gating only the vertical channel, the risk a Level II must control is:
options: The air point will drift off screen during the scan
A defect whose signal phase lies close to the lift-off direction loses amplitude or disappears — separation must be proven on reference notches
The instrument can no longer measure conductivity at any frequency
Lift-off will reverse direction and read as a crack
checkQuestions.explanation: Vertical-only evaluation discards everything parallel to lift-off. Frequency is chosen — and verified on the reference standard, through a shim simulating maximum coating — to keep defect responses angularly separated from lift-off.

checkQuestions.question: When measuring nonconductive coating thickness on an aluminum part by the eddy current (lift-off) method, ASTM E376 practice requires calibration:
options: In air, since the air point anchors the lift-off locus
On any convenient conductive block, since lift-off is material-independent
On the bare base metal of the same alloy and geometry, using certified nonconductive shims spanning the expected range
At a single mid-range thickness, because the response is linear
checkQuestions.explanation: The lift-off locus depends on the substrate's conductivity, curvature, and thickness, and the response is nonlinear — so calibration uses matching bare base metal with certified shims across the range, verified periodically.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, ET Level II Technique Course §5.2 (coupling — liftoff)
ASNT SNT-TC-1A (2024), Eddy Current Testing method — Level II training topic basis
ASTM E376, Standard Practice for Measuring Coating Thickness by Magnetic-Field or Eddy-Current (Electromagnetic) Testing Methods
ASTM E1004, Standard Test Method for Determining Electrical Conductivity Using the Electromagnetic (Eddy-Current) Method (lift-off compensation provisions)
